items.description: Public type Greeter with greet(name:) -> String returning "Hello, <name>!" and greet() -> String returning "Hello, world!"

rules_checked.reasoning: Item declares kind 'type' and describes a concrete struct/class Greeter holding two methods (greet(name:) and greet()) with fixed behavior — this is a plain data/behavior type, not a protocol, service, or view; 'type' correctly matches what it is.

Target Sources/Greeter/Greeter.swift places the type inside the 'Greeter' target declared in Package.swift (.target(name: "Greeter")), which is the module's own home — correct location for the primary public API of the Greeter package.

This is a standalone library type with no view/view-model layering in this minimal walking-skeleton package; there is no domain logic being placed in a view or data access in a view model. Not applicable/violated — the type is the entire service-equivalent surface for this trivial package.

No existing type exists to extend (Sources/Greeter currently only contains the directory placeholder, no prior Greeter.swift). A new type is warranted since this is the first and sole public type for the package; granularity is appropriate (single-responsibility greeting type, no split needed for two trivial methods).
The item introduces a concrete type only, no protocol. No speculative abstraction is proposed, so this rule is satisfied trivially — nothing to reject.

Type name 'Greeter' is a noun describing its role (Swift API Design Guidelines: types are nouns); methods greet(name:) and greet() are verbs describing the action, following Swift naming conventions for methods. Names carry appropriate semantics for a 'type' kind.
No imports or cross-module dependencies are introduced by this item; it is a self-contained type within its own target with no forbidden import smuggled in.
